posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked:
A couple of nice bottles over the weekend to celebrate another lap around the sun for Christine.

2010 Jean-Marc Bouley Pommard Rugiens 1er
Discussing Bouley with Jeremy during the week, he opined that their Fremiers is often regarded above the Rugiens. Only having access to the latter, I thought that it was high time to bust one.
This is quite an elegant rendition, crunchy red fruits, lovely floral perfume, quite a seductive, glossy palate. Really lovely and a wine to disprove the long outdated theory that Pommards are hard and blocky.
2012 Hubert Lignier Chambolle-Musigny VV
Surprisingly open and surprisingly fine, with nicely handled oak subsumed by the silky, lacy dark fruits. Lovely perfume, all rose garden and porcini. Delicious and relatively youthful but offering plenty of interest today.

posts.name: Joel Deutsch
posts.cooked: Really good notes. I haven’t had many Bouley Pommards, but after your comments I will do my best to run down a few for the cellar!

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the notes Kent.

Bouley is an outstanding maker and with warmer, earlier harvests, Pommard is a village that is really worth exploring.

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked:
Really good notes. I haven’t had many Bouley Pommards, but after your comments I will do my best to run down a few for the cellar!

Bouley make very good Pommard (and Volnays) as do Voillot-Guillemard.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Thanks for the note. Interestingly, I opened a 2010 Fremiers about 6 months ago for my zoom group, and found it pretty tough and unyielding. I probably won’t touch another for 5-8 years.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the notes. For me, the Bouley wines are markedly better from 2012 on. I also think that despite recent hype for the fremiers, the volnay caillerets is particularly special.
Alex

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the notes. For me, the Bouley wines are markedly better from 2012 on. I also think that despite recent hype for the fremiers, the volnay caillerets is particularly special.

Well that’s good news. Have a few 15s kicking around.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: I’ve been impressed by pommard lately. Anyone have a comte armaud of recent vintage?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the notes. For me, the Bouley wines are markedly better from 2012 on. I also think that despite recent hype for the fremiers, the volnay caillerets is particularly special.
Alex

Having done a big vertical with Thomas earlier this year, I’m not sure I agree that there was a huge shift in 2012. Progress has been incremental, year on year, since Thomas took over in the early 2000s, and if I had to single out one step change, I’d point to the difference between 2008 and 2010, which is largely to do with better sanitation for used barrels. I do agree, however, that what Thomas did in the hail vintages of 2012, 2013 and 2014 is very impressive! Since then, he has extended élevage and further refined cooperage choices, so the progress continues. Thomas himself considers 2010, 2015 and 2019 to be the best he has made to date, FWIW.

I’ve tried to highlight how good Fremiers is lately, because Rugiens and Caillerets both tended to get all the love due to name recognition, whereas I think Fremiers is, for Thomas and given the locations of his holdings, the better wine: it’s deeper, more complete, and more multidimensional. His vines in Caillerets were planted in 2004 vs Fermiers in 1970, which makes a difference, too (though at this level of viticulture, vine age matters less I think). Caillerets tends to be the ripest / highest alcohol wine in the cellar. But the location of the parcels is the key: his Rugiens is right at the top of the vineyard to the north, with white soils and a steep slope, producing an ethereal, linear wine that’s very far from the stereotype of Rugiens-Bas with its red soils and very full, muscular wines; and his Caillerets is similarly situated, by the road to the north, not from the sector near the Clos des 60 Ouvrées which has redder, richer soils and makes the most complete wines in this big climat. That’s not a criticism of those wines, I hasten to add, as I love them both: but when you compare those parcels with his plot of Fremiers, it makes complete sense that they should be different in the ways that they are, and one could say, I think, that his Fremiers is “more Rugiens” than his Rugiens.

The real values in the range, however, are certainly the Hautes-Côtes (from a beautiful enclave on the plateau just above Volnay, surrounded by trees) and the Volnay Clos de la Cave, and with regional and communal bottlings at that level we are already spoiled.

posts.cooked:

Interesting. I love all the Bouley wines, and I’m obviously nowhere near your level of expertise, but I’m not sure that things like vine age and expositions really explain differences in the Bouley’s various wines, or lead to a conclusion that one is superior to the others. He doesn’t seem to follow a formula and takes what he’s given, so there can be significant differences year to year. I’m thinking of 2015, where the fremiers is whole cluster and the rugiens is de-stemmed, and then in 2016 the rugiens does have some stems (can’t remember what he did with the fremiers in 2016). The same dynamic is true of the caillerets vs. the clos de chenes. So there are significant winemaking differences not just among various wines but from year to year. These are the kinds of things that seem to be part of the spirit chez Bouley over the last several years. For me, those stand out from 2012 on.

The one I think of as having the most name cache is actually not the caillerets or the rugiens, but the clos des chenes. That is the one i typically hear people refer to as “best.” As for real value, the fremiers is the only one that I think of as very expensive, and while I like the clos de la cave, I’ve always thought of the carelles as worth the moderate step-up in price.

In any event, I think of the wines across the board at Bouley as among my favorites in burgundy. I’m happy to buy them all and I hope you stop writing about them! I bought a bunch more of the fremiers from Howard Ripley when you said you were going to do a vertical on the theory that they would start becoming harder to get and more expensive.
Alex

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Interesting. I love all the Bouley wines, and I’m obviously nowhere near your level of expertise, but I’m not sure that things like vine age and expositions really explain differences in the Bouley’s various wines, or lead to a conclusion that one is superior to the others. He doesn’t seem to follow a formula and takes what he’s given, so there can be significant differences year to year. I’m thinking of 2015, where the fremiers is whole cluster and the rugiens is de-stemmed, and then in 2016 the rugiens does have some stems (can’t remember what he did with the fremiers in 2016). The same dynamic is true of the caillerets vs. the clos de chenes. So there are significant winemaking differences not just among various wines but from year to year. These are the kinds of things that seem to be part of the spirit chez Bouley over the last several years. For me, those stand out from 2012 on.

The one I think of as having the most name cache is actually not the caillerets or the rugiens, but the clos des chenes. That is the one i typically hear people refer to as “best.” As for real value, the fremiers is the only one that I think of as very expensive, and while I like the clos de la cave, I’ve always thought of the carelles as worth the moderate step-up in price.

In any event, I think of the wines across the board at Bouley as among my favorites in burgundy. I’m happy to buy them all and I hope you stop writing about them! I bought a bunch more of the fremiers from Howard Ripley when you said you were going to do a vertical on the theory that they would start becoming harder to get and more expensive.
Alex

Ah, on the contrary, I think expositions, soils, mesoclimate and vine age / genetics explain most of the differences! That is certainly what Thomas is aiming for. As for stems, there is in fact something of a formula, in that he retains the majority of stems for his clay-rich sites, whereas the stonier sites are mostly or entirely destemmed. Rugiens, being from the high and stony part of the vineyard, is thus generally mostly destemmed, whereas Fremiers is mostly not (in 2016, he kept some 30% stems for Rugiens because the wine was unusually rich). But he does this because he believes it’s complementary to the differences between the sites, not in any attempt to efface the differences. He began using whole bunches, inspired by Nicolas Rossignol, in 2003, and he settled on this approach of using them mostly for his clay-rich terroirs in the years that followed, well before 2012.

That is interesting what you say about Clos des Chênes, that hadn’t really occurred to me. I guess the people assuming that are Lafarge drinkers!

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
That is interesting what you say about Clos des Chênes, that hadn’t really occurred to me. I guess the people assuming that are Lafarge drinkers!

Chenes is the Bouley wine I value most in the portfolio; every time I’ve tasted through, either at the winery or Paulee, it stands out. Right up there with Fremiers for me.

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked: Wonderful discussion gents and perhaps the hype on Bouley may be gaining traction. They have been a hard sell in the restaurant, but I will continue to purchase them awaiting the stampede!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Wonderful discussion gents and perhaps the hype on Bouley may be gaining traction. They have been a hard sell in the restaurant, but I will continue to purchase them awaiting the stampede!

Hopefully it isn’t “hype”, but rather recognition for one of the Côte d’Or’s most serious viticulturists and winemakers—who now has a twenty-vintage track record of excellence to his credit!

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked:

Hopefully it isn’t “hype”, but rather recognition for one of the Côte d’Or’s most serious viticulturists and winemakers—who now has a twenty-vintage track record of excellence to his credit!

Agreed. Greatly appreciate your input here William. It’s humbling to start a thread and come away learning so much. I will be having a fair crack at Bouley’s '19s when they are released.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
They have been a hard sell in the restaurant, but I will continue to purchase them awaiting the stampede!

Why do you suppose they are a hard sell, Kent? Just lack of name recognition? Are people generally happy when they open them? I’m shocked that the wines haven’t taken off in the US. Can’t figure out why. But I intend to take a bottle of the ‘15 caillerets to any tasting I go to post-covid in the hopes of changing minds.
A

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked:
They have been a hard sell in the restaurant, but I will continue to purchase them awaiting the stampede!

Why do you suppose they are a hard sell, Kent? Just lack of name recognition? Are people generally happy when they open them? I’m shocked that the wines haven’t taken off in the US. Can’t figure out why. But I intend to take a bottle of the ‘15 caillerets to any tasting I go to post-covid in the hopes of changing minds.
A

Not sure really. Often I find if customers are looking to spend that kind of money on a Burgundy, they will instinctively look to Nuits. Ultimately for many customers Volnay or pommard does not have the cachet of Chambolle or Vosne, without fully appreciating what’s in the glass. For me, I often look towards the Volnays or Pommards or Mercureys as these often can provide the best bang for the buck.
